section.name: Introduction
section.p:
Commons Geometry contains a number of methods for manipulating solid, 3D dimensional figures. These geometric figures can be combined in various ways to produce new figures. In this tutorial, we will explore these features by using them to construct a 3D model of a teapot from scratch. The image below shows the result of our efforts. The final code for this tutorial can be found in the
TeapotBuilder class.
NOTE: Unless otherwise noted, all images used in this tutorial have been rendered using Blender.

section.name: Getting Started
section.p: The first step we will take on our journey is the creation of a class encapsulating our teapot-construction logic, aptly named TeapotBuilder. The constructor will accept only a single argument: an instance of DoublePrecisionContext. This is a ubiquitous type in Commons Geometry and is used to provide a method of comparing floating point numbers without being susceptible to small floating point errors introduced during computations. Typically, users of Commons Geometry will construct a single instance of this type for use by multiple objects throughout an entire operation, or even an entire application. Since we don't want our class to assume such a heavy responsibility, we will simply accept a DoublePrecisionContext in the constructor.
Next, we will create a stub method for our teapot-building code. We will fill in this method as we work through the tutorial.

We have chosen the RegionBSPTree3D type for both the construction of the teapot geometry as well as the return value. This is the primary type in Commons Geometry for manipulating solid geometries. (There are similar such types for each space and dimension supported by the library, such as RegionBSPTree2D for 2D Euclidean space and RegionBSPTree2S for 2D spherical space.) This type uses a binary space partitioning (BSP) tree to represent arbitrary regions of space, including regions of infinite size. This is accomplished by recursively dividing a space in two by partitioning planes (or "hyperplanes", to use the more general term). The spaces on either side of the planes are then given the labels "inside" or "outside". A region is then composed of all of the "inside" portions of the BSP tree.

A major benefit of using BSP trees to represent regions is that it allows us to perform boolean operations such as union, intersection, difference, and xor on arbitrary regions. This is exactly how we will build our teapot geometry: by using the BSP tree boolean operations to combine simpler shapes into the final product.

Before constructing our first geometry, we need to handle one more bit of housekeeping, namely, how to view our work. The easiest way to do this is to export our geometries using a common 3D file format, such as STL, and view the model in a 3D modeling program. I enjoy working with Blender so that is the modeling program I have chosen to use for this tutorial. However any program able to load and display 3D models should work. To create our geometry files, we will use the 3D file writing capabilities built in to Commons Geometry and accessible through the IO3D convenience class. See the GeometryFormat3D enum for a list of supported file formats.
With this bit of code place, we can now run our TeapotBuilder, load the teapot.stl file in our 3D modeling program, and see our first pass at a teapot!

Well, that is a bit underwhelming to say the least. The RegionBSPTree3D instance that we are returning from our TeapotBuilder is completely empty so there is nothing to look at. We'll start adding content next.

section.name: Building the Parts
section.p: Now that we have a basic code skeleton in place, we can begin constructing the various parts of our teapot. We will have four main parts: 1) the body, 2) the lid, 3) the handle, and 4) the spout. We will create private methods in our TeapotBuilder for each part.

subsection.name: The Body
subsection.p: We will start on the teapot body by creating a new private method named buildBody and referencing it in the primary buildTeapot method.

You'll notice that we've also started using one of the boolean operations mentioned earlier: union. The line teapot.union(body) computes the union of the teapot BSP tree with the body BSP tree and stores the result back into the caller, teapot. This is how we will merge all of the parts of our teapot into a single 3D region. Now, let's start building the body.
Unlike some fancier models, our teapot is going to have a simple, rounded body. We will start by creating a sphere and go from there. Luckily, Commons Geometry provides a Sphere class to help us.

We now have a Sphere instance centered on the origin with a radius of 1. However, our instance represents an analytic sphere, meaning it is does not contain any flat surfaces. This is great for many use cases, but we need flat surfaces in order to construct a BSP tree for our region. To convert from our mathematically perfect sphere abstraction to a BSP tree sphere approximation, we will use the Sphere.toTree() method. This method accepts a single argument that determines the number of facets that will be used in the approximation. The documentation explains the details of the conversion. For our purposes, we will use the argument 4, which will give us a BSP tree with 2048 facets.
subsection.source: Sphere sphere = Sphere.from(Vector3D.ZERO, 1, precision); RegionBSPTree3D body = sphere.toTree(4);
subsection.p: This finally gives us something we can look at in our 3D modeling program.

Our next step is to tweak the sphere to make it more teapot-like. First, we will squash it vertically a little to make it less of a perfect sphere. Our tool of choice for this squashing exercise is the AffineTransformMatrix3D class, which we will be using quite frequently in the remainder of this tutorial. This class represents a 4x4 transform matrix that can be used to perform affine transformations in 3D space. In short, it lets us perform operations like translate, scale, and rotate on geometries. Here, we will use it to scale down our sphere approximation along the z-axis, while keeping the x and y scales the same.
Our sphere now looks flattened a bit.

Note that our choice to scale along the z-axis (as opposed to the x or y axes) was completely arbitrary; our sphere was entirely symmetrical and we had no definition of what was "up" and what was "down" for our teapot. Now that we have performed our first non-symmetrical operation, we should officially declare the orientation of our teapot: henceforth the positive z-axis will be "up" and the the positive x-axis will be "forward" (the direction that the spout is pointing) in teapot-space. Keeping this orientation in mind will help us when working through later transformations.

Our teapot is now pleasantly flattened but it is still very likely to roll off tables and create all manner of messes. To solve this shortcoming, we will chop off part of the bottom to make a flat base for the teapot to sit on. Another way to picture this is that we want to remove the part of the body that lies below a certain plane. As such, we can define this plane, construct a region of infinite size with that plane as the "top", and compute the difference between our flattened sphere and the infinite region.
There's a lot going on in just a few lines there so let's go through it step by step.
subsection.source: Plane bottomPlane = Planes.fromPointAndNormal( Vector3D.of(0, 0, -0.6), Vector3D.Unit.PLUS_Z, precision);
subsection.p: Here we construct our plane from a point lying in the plane, the plane normal, and our ever-present DoublePrecisionContext. We chose a point that is directly "below" the origin per our previous definition of the teapot orientation and thus will intersect the bottom of the teapot. The normal points "up", along the positive z axis.

This may well be the most confusing line in this section. This line constructs a PlaneConvexSubset that represents all of the points lying in the plane we created just before. These seem like equivalent concepts but they're slightly different. The plane can be pictured as a container defining what points are available, while the convex subset selects a convex group of those points. Convex subsets include triangles, convex polygons, half-spaces, and, as in this case, every single last point in the entire plane (i.e., the "span").
